items being removed — so `## Todo` carried zombie entries
describing work already underway. The fix is a rule, not a
one-off cleanup: when a `## Todo` item is picked up — its `##
In Progress` ladder created, or an existing ladder *re-scoped*
to absorb it — the entry is deleted in the same commit. `## In
Progress` is the sole record until close-out, then it moves to
`## Done`. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current In-Progress
work is a process bug; the pre-commit checklist gets a
catch-line for it.

## feat: chid+co -s/--scope flag (0.49.0-2.2)

The code half of the `0.49.0-2` rollout (`0.49.0-2.3` will sweep
the docs). Replaces today's `-R .,.claude` / `-R . -R .claude`
multi-repo forms with a roles flag (`-s code | bot | code,bot`),
keeps `-R` for single-path operation, and lets the two compose
as a workspace-root override. See `### Cycle re-scope`'s
`--scope` bullet for the wider plan; the two `###` subsections
below capture the design pivots that landed in this commit.

### Design: `-R` and `-s` compose rather than conflict

The chores plan had `-R` dropped, with `--scope=<path>` covering
single-repo via `Scope::Single`. On review the design walked
through three iterations:

- **Drop `-R`.** `--scope=./foo` covers single-path via
  `Scope::Single`; one flag, the `./` prefix disambiguates from
  the role keywords.
- **Mutually-exclusive `-R` + `-s`.** Keep `-R` for paths, add
  `-s` for roles, `conflicts_with` between them. Familiar `-R`
  preserved; redundant on the path side.
- **Composing `-R` + `-s`** (chosen). `-R` overrides the
  workspace root (replaces `find_workspace_root()`), `-s`
  selects sides (replaces `default_scope(...)`). Together:
  `-R <ws> -s <roles>` resolves the roles within `<ws>`. Each
  flag alone reads as today's behavior: `-R foo` → `[foo]`, no
  flag → `[.]`. The net-new expressivity is the combined form
  (e.g. `vc-x1 chid -R ../foo -s bot` → `[../foo/.claude]`).
  `-s` is keyword-only today (`parse_scope_roles` rejects paths
  with a hint at `-R`); `-s <path>` and the `-s <path>,roles`
  workspace-root override are queued as `## Todo` (a future
  `Scope::RolesAt { root, sides }` variant, probably). "Drop
  `-R` once `-s` is established" is a separate `## Todo` for
  after the migration period — kept for backwards-compat now.

### Design: `CommonArgs::resolve_repos(&self)` helper

`common::resolve_repos(repo, scope)` takes `Option<&Path>` +
`Option<&Scope>` — the standard "borrowed unsized" convention
(`&Path`, not `&PathBuf`). At each call site that produces an
asymmetric `c.repo.as_deref()` / `c.scope.as_ref()` pair — four
times across the subcommand bodies, with another four to come in
`0.49.0-3..-6`'s `TryFrom<&XxxArgs>` impls. A method on
`CommonArgs` localizes the conversion ceremony to one place so
callers read `c.resolve_repos()?`, and a new
`notes/rust-idioms.md` carries the `as_deref` vs `as_ref`
explainer the doc-comment links to. The free function stays as
the reusable primitive (a future `finalize --scope` /
`push --scope` calls it directly, not through `CommonArgs`).

## docs: chid+co -s/--scope flag (0.49.0-2.3)

The docs half of the `0.49.0-2` rollout. `README.md`'s
`### Multi-repo queries` section is rewritten to lead with `-s` /
`--scope` — every example switches from `-R .,.claude` /
`-R . -R .claude` to `-s code,bot` (the former no longer parse
since `-R` is now single-path). The `-R PATH` examples retain
the single-repo cases (`-R .`, `-R .claude`, `-R ../other`) and
the workspace-root-override compose form (`-R ../other -s
code,bot`); the trailing paragraph points users at the queued
`-s <path>` / `-s <path>,roles` `## Todo` items. `ARCHITECTURE.md`
gets `resolve_repos` added to the `common.rs` helper list with a
one-line note on what it composes. The `sync` flag table at the
bottom of `README.md` keeps the older `-R` repeatable/comma-list
wording — that's still the live behavior on `sync` and is gated
behind its own `## Todo` (drop `-R` from `CommonArgs` once the
migration period ends).

content.content:
108	## Commit Message Style
109	
110	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
111	a version suffix:
112	
113	```
114	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
115	```
116	
117	- **Title**: ≤50 chars (the "50" of the git 50/72 rule), a short
118	  summary of *what* changed. The version suffix counts toward the
119	  50 — the descriptive part gets whatever's left after `<type>: `
120	  and ` (<version>)`, so favor terse phrasings (`port X to Context`
121	  over `X → Context + XParams`) when the names run long. Common
122	  types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
123	- **Body line width**: wrap every body line at ≤72 cols (the "72"
124	  of the 50/72 rule); bullet continuations indent two spaces.
125	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
126	  terse bullet list — one bullet per file changed, the file plus a
127	  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). This list
128	  **is the source of truth** for the cycle's mechanical change
129	  record; the chores section carries the narrative + design, not a
130	  copy of it. Keep bullets terse — a scannable index, not prose;
131	  promote any "why" to a chores `###` subsection.
132	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
133	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
134	  in-session work, not code changes.
135	- Examples:
136	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
137	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
138	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
139	
140	## Pre-commit Requirements
141	
142	### User approval
143	
144	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
145	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
146	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
147	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.

file.content:
## Commit Message Style

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

- **Title**: ≤50 chars (the "50" of the git 50/72 rule), a short
  summary of *what* changed. The version suffix counts toward the
  50 — the descriptive part gets whatever's left after `<type>: `
  and ` (<version>)`, so favor terse phrasings (`port X to Context`
  over `X → Context + XParams`) when the names run long. Common
  types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **Body line width**: wrap every body line at ≤72 cols (the "72"
  of the 50/72 rule); bullet continuations indent two spaces.
- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
  terse bullet list — one bullet per file changed, the file plus a
  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). This list
  **is the source of truth** for the cycle's mechanical change
  record; the chores section carries the narrative + design, not a
  copy of it. Keep bullets terse — a scannable index, not prose;
  promote any "why" to a chores `###` subsection.
- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
  in-session work, not code changes.
- Examples:
  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`

## Pre-commit Requirements

### User approval

Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.

content.content:
329	### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
330	
331	A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
332	header — see below), a short intro paragraph (what changed and
333	*why*, a notch above file-list granularity), and any `###`
334	design subsections. It does **not** carry a per-file edit list —
335	that lives in the commit message body, which is the source of
336	truth for "what changed mechanically" (immutable, `git
337	show`-able, naturally scoped to the commit). The chores section
338	is the source of truth for the design thinking; the two
339	cross-link, neither restates the other.
340	
341	When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
342	hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its

### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), a short intro paragraph (what changed and
*why*, a notch above file-list granularity), and any `###`
design subsections. It does **not** carry a per-file edit list —
that lives in the commit message body, which is the source of
truth for "what changed mechanically" (immutable, `git
show`-able, naturally scoped to the commit). The chores section
is the source of truth for the design thinking; the two
cross-link, neither restates the other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)

Commits: [[3]]

content.content:
624	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
625	
626	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
627	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
628	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
629	comment exists, not that it be long.
630	
631	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
632	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
633	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
634	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
635	non-obvious WHY.
636	
637	**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
638	a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
639	paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
640	that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:
641	
642	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
643	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
644	- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
645	  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).
646	
647	**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
648	`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
649	into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
650	any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
651	its own line in the rendered help.
652	
653	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
654	
655	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
656	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
657	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
658

file.content:
### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

content.text:
Chores-10 paragraph updated.

Now CLAUDE.md suggestions. The Shape rule (line 637–645) is already in place but lives under `### Doc comments on every file, function, and method`, which buries it. Three places in CLAUDE.md describe what amounts to the same intro+bullets shape:

| Location | What it covers |
|---|---|
| **L637 Shape rule** | Doc comments + chores + todo |
| **L117–134 Commit Message Style bullets** | Commit body (intro + file-by-file bullets) |
| **L329–339 Chores section content** | Chores intro + design `###`s; "no per-file edit list" |

Concrete proposals:

### 1. Expand the Shape rule to cover commits + Done (smallest fix)

**L637–645 change** — replace:

```
**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).
```

with:

```
**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences) that explains the *why*
or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet list for the
details. Wrap body lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
indent two spaces). Avoid long prose paragraphs — they read as
a wall of text and hide the structure that bullets make
scannable. Same shape applies to:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
  50-col title rule is the commit-specific add-on; see
  [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style).
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an entry needs
  more than one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).

Bullet *content* differs by site: commit bodies use **file-by-file**
bullets (the mechanical edit list — source of truth for what
changed); chores / todo / done use **conceptual** bullets (design
points, structural details — not a copy of the commit's edit list).
```

### 2. Slim the Commit Message Style bullets to defer to Shape

**L117–134 change** — replace the four bullets (`Title`, `Body line width`, `App-repo body`, `Session-repo body`) with three bullets:

```
- **Title**: ≤50 chars (the "50" of the git 50/72 rule), a short
  summary of *what* changed. The version suffix counts toward the
  50 — the descriptive part gets whatever's left after `<type>: `
  and ` (<version>)`, so favor terse phrasings (`port X to Context`
  over `X → Context + XParams`) when the names run long. Common
  types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **Body**: intro + bullets shape (the "72" of the 50/72 rule
  applies; see [the Shape rule](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method)).
  App-repo bullets are **file-by-file** — one bullet per file
  changed, the file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). This list **is the source of
  truth** for the cycle's mechanical change record; chores
  carries the narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
- **Session-repo body**: same shape; bullets describe in-session
  activity rather than code changes.
```

### 3. Cross-link from the Chores section content rule

**L331–333 tweak** — change the phrasing so it points at the Shape rule instead of re-defining:

Current:
```
A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), a short intro paragraph (what changed and
*why*, a notch above file-list granularity), and any `###`
design subsections.
```

Proposed:
```
A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then the intro + bullets
[Shape](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method)
(intro explains the *why* at a notch above file-list
granularity; bullets are **conceptual** design / structural
points, never a per-file edit list), and any `###` design
subsections.
```

---

**Optional larger move (not recommended unless you want it):** rename the section housing Shape from `### Doc comments on every file, function, and method` to a more generic `### Prose shape and doc-comment rule`, since Shape now governs four prose surfaces, not just `.rs` doc comments. Or promote Shape to its own `### Prose shape: intro + bullets` subsection above Doc comments. Either reshuffles anchors and the per-commit anchor-back-ref rule kicks in.

Want me to apply 1 + 2 + 3 (light touch) as part of this `-2.3` sub-step, or scope it to a separate cycle? Either way I'll wait for your call before editing CLAUDE.md.

file.content:
## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.

## Commit Message Style

  session because session data updates continuously.

## Prose form

Long-lived prose on this project follows one shape: a short intro
that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
structure that bullets make scannable.

Surfaces that use this shape:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
  [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style).
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md` — see
  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.

Bullet *content* differs by surface:

- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
  mechanical edit list.
- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
  cases, invariants).

## Commit Message Style

header — see below), a short intro paragraph (what changed and
*why*, a notch above file-list granularity), and any `###`
design subsections. It does **not** carry a per-file edit list —
that lives in the commit message body, which is the source of
truth for "what changed mechanically" (immutable, `git
show`-able, naturally scoped to the commit). The chores section
is the source of truth for the design thinking; the two
cross-link, neither restates the other.

content.text: Now the Doc comments section — slim by removing the Shape paragraph (it lives in Prose form now), keep clap-derive.

input.old_string:
### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

input.new_string:
## docs: chid+co -s/--scope flag (0.49.0-2.3)

The docs half of the `0.49.0-2` rollout. Code shipped in `-2.2`
but `README.md` still showed the pre-`-R`-single-path multi-repo
forms (`-R .,.claude` / `-R . -R .claude`) — anyone reading the
user-facing docs would be steered into invocations that no longer
parse. The cycle also surfaced a process drift: chores, todo, and
commit bodies all describe what amounts to the same intro+bullets
shape, but CLAUDE.md codified it in three almost-but-not-quite
places. Unified into one `## Prose form` section as the cycle's
second half. See [§ Design: Prose form unification](#design-prose-form-unification).

- `README.md` `### Multi-repo queries` rewritten to lead with
  `-s code,bot`; every example updated to the new form.
- `-R PATH` retained as the single-path escape hatch (`-R .`,
  `-R .claude`, `-R ../other`) and as the workspace-root
  override that composes with `-s` (`-R ../other -s code,bot`).
- Trailing paragraph forward-links to the `-s <path>` and
  `-s <path>,roles` future-Todos so readers see the deferred
  expressivity.
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` gains `resolve_repos` in the `common.rs`
  helper list with a one-line note on what it composes.
- `sync` flag table at the bottom of `README.md` left alone —
  `sync` still has the old repeatable/comma-list `-R`; its
  migration is queued under "Drop `-R` from `CommonArgs`".
- CLAUDE.md: new top-level `## Prose form` section is the single
  source of truth for the intro+bullets shape; `## Commit Message
  Style`, `### Chores section content`, and `### Doc comments…`
  slim to one-line references.

### Design: Prose form unification

Before this cycle CLAUDE.md described the same shape (short
intro + `-` bullet list, ≤72 wrap) three times — once inside
`### Doc comments…` (covering doc comments + chores + todo),
once inside `## Commit Message Style` (commit bodies, with the
50-col title add-on), once inside `### Chores section content`
("intro paragraph, what changed and *why*"). The drift between
them was small but real: only the Doc-comments copy carried the
explicit list of surfaces; only the Commit-Message copy carried
the file-by-file bullet rule; Done entries weren't mentioned
anywhere.

Three alternatives considered:

- **Expand the existing Shape rule's list** to add commits +
  Done. Smallest change; leaves the rule buried in `### Doc
  comments…` where it's hard to find when writing a commit body
  or chores section.
- **Cross-link three definitions to each other.** Each site
  redefines the shape but pointers run between them. Reads
  consistent but still drifts: three copies of the same
  paragraph still need three edits to stay in sync.
- **Promote to a single top-level `## Prose form` section**
  (chosen). The shape is defined once; the three (now four —
  Done entries) consumer sites carry only the surface-specific
  add-ons (50-col title for commits, file-by-file bullets for
  commits, `Commits:` line + design `###`s for chores, etc.).
  Anchors stay stable on the existing sections so notes/ refs
  don't churn.

The chosen form also distinguishes bullet *content* per
surface: commit-body bullets are file-by-file (the source of
truth for the mechanical edit list — `git show` is the record);
chores / todo / done bullets are conceptual (design points,
structural notes — never a copy of the commit's edit list);
doc-comment bullets are whatever structure fits.

Design:
[chores-10.md](chores-10.md#chore-open-0490--finish-migration-a-0490-0)
+ the 0.49.0-1 re-scope subsection; prior `--scope` design in
[chores-06](chores-06.md#generalize---scope-across-commands-0400),
[chores-07](chores-07.md#--scope-enum-refactor-0420).

   - 0.49.0-0 plan + version bump + chores section + ladder (done)
   - 0.49.0-1 options_flags extraction — relocate `CommonArgs`
     → `options_flags/common_args.rs`. Kept separate; no `-1`
     close-out commit.
     - 0.49.0-1.1 the relocation + all four importers (done)
     - 0.49.0-1.2 docs: slim ARCHITECTURE.md; start chores-10 (done)
   - 0.49.0-2 `-R`/`--repo` → `-s`/`--scope` for `chid` /
     `desc` / `list` / `show`.
     - 0.49.0-2.1 open the step (done)
       - tidy `## Todo` (drop the two now-in-progress items).
       - add CLAUDE.md rule — a picked-up `## Todo` item is
         deleted when it goes `## In Progress`.
       - backfill `-1.1` / `-1.2` chores `Commits:` refs.
     - 0.49.0-2.2 the rollout — code (done)
       - kept `-R` as single path (`Option<PathBuf>`); added
         `-s`/`--scope` (`Option<Scope>` via
         `parse_scope_roles`, keyword-only — `-s <path>` is a
         future Todo).
       - the two compose (no `conflicts_with`): `-R` overrides
         workspace root, `-s` selects sides within it. New
         `common::resolve_repos(repo, scope)` does the match;
         `for_each_repo` takes a resolved `Vec<PathBuf>`.
       - defaults preserve today: no flag → `[.]`, `-R foo`
         alone → `[foo]`.
       - scope: four subcommand bodies + `--help`; tests;
         CLAUDE.md `chid -R .,.claude -L` → `chid -s code,bot -L`.
     - 0.49.0-2.3 docs (current)
       - `README.md` `### Multi-repo queries` rewritten to lead
         with `-s code,bot`; every example updated. The prior
         `-R .,.claude` / `-R . -R .claude` forms no longer
         parse since `-R` is now single-path.
       - `-R PATH` retained for single-path use and as the
         workspace-root override that composes with `-s`
         (`-R ../other -s code,bot`).
       - `ARCHITECTURE.md`: `resolve_repos` added to the
         `common.rs` helper list with a one-line note on what
         it composes.
       - `notes/` swept; no stale `-R .,.claude` for these four.
       - CLAUDE.md: new top-level `## Prose form` section
         unifies the intro+bullets shape across commit bodies /
         chores / todo / done / doc comments; slim
         `## Commit Message Style`, `### Chores section
         content`, `### Doc comments…` to reference it.
   - 0.49.0-3 chid Context+Params port + introduce `CommonParams`
   - 0.49.0-4 desc Context+Params port
   - 0.49.0-5 list Context+Params port
   - 0.49.0-6 show Context+Params port (`TryFrom`, `FileLimit` parse)
   - 0.49.0 close-out — drop suffix, todo→Done (Context+Params
     port 12/12 + CommonArgs sweep), README + ARCHITECTURE.md

`notes/todo.md` reformatted. Re-rendered for review:

```
   - 0.49.0-2.3 docs (current)
     - `README.md` `### Multi-repo queries` rewritten to lead
       with `-s code,bot`; every example updated. The prior

       (`-R ../other -s code,bot`).
     - `ARCHITECTURE.md`: `resolve_repos` added to the
       `common.rs` helper list with a one-line note on what
       it composes.
     - `notes/` swept; no stale `-R .,.claude` for these four.
     - CLAUDE.md: new top-level `## Prose form` section
       unifies the intro+bullets shape across commit bodies /
       chores / todo / done / doc comments; slim
       `## Commit Message Style`, `### Chores section
       content`, `### Doc comments…` to reference it.
```

Now you can see the seam visually: bullets 1–4 are the `-s/--scope` docs rollout; bullet 5 is the prose-form unification. They share no semantic ground — the prose-form work emerged from process drift caught while writing the `-2.3` chores section.

**Recommendation: split into `-2.3` + `-2.4`.**

- **`-2.3`** = the original docs sweep: `README.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/` sweep, `notes/chores-10.md` `-2.3` section (no Design subsection), `notes/todo.md` marker update, `Cargo.toml` → `0.49.0-2.3`.
- **`-2.4`** = the prose-form unification: `CLAUDE.md` (new `## Prose form` + the three slim-downs), `notes/chores-10.md` adds `## chore: prose-form unification (0.49.0-2.4)` with the Design subsection, `notes/todo.md` adds a `-2.4 (current)` ladder bullet, `Cargo.toml` → `0.49.0-2.4`.

**Why split:**

- Bisection clarity — one logical change per sub-step.
- The chores narrative reads cleanly: `## docs: -s/--scope sweep` is the rollout completion; `## chore: prose-form unification` is the separate process improvement.
- Either is bisectable to a small surface (no mixed-cause regressions to untangle).
- The cost is only mechanical: split the WC before the next commit.

**Why not split (one-paragraph case for keeping in `-2.3`):**

- The two halves were edited together; no actual commit yet, so the working copy already has them mixed. Splitting is real work.
- The "docs" `<type>:` on `-2.3` would honestly cover the CLAUDE.md change too — it's all documentation, just two themes.

**Mechanics if you say "split":** I'd revert the prose-form bits in the WC (CLAUDE.md, the `### Design:` subsection in chores-10, the prose-form bullet in todo.md), run cargo cycle, propose `-2.3`'s title/body for push approval. After `-2.3` lands, re-apply the prose-form changes, bump Cargo.toml to `0.49.0-2.4`, add the `-2.4` chores section + ladder bullet, run cargo cycle, propose `-2.4`'s title/body for push.

My vote: split. Your call.

Splitting. Reverting the prose-form bits from the WC so `-2.3` is just the `-s/--scope` docs sweep, then I'll prep `-2.4` after `-2.3` lands.

Reverts to make:
1. CLAUDE.md — remove the new `## Prose form` section + restore the three slimmed sections.
2. `notes/chores-10.md` — drop the `### Design: Prose form unification` subsection + slim the `-2.3` intro and bullets.
3. `notes/todo.md` — drop the CLAUDE.md bullet from `-2.3`; add a pending `-2.4` ladder bullet; flip `-2.3` to `(done)` per pre-commit checklist item 6.

Starting with CLAUDE.md.

input.old_string:
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.

## Prose form

Long-lived prose on this project follows one shape: a short intro
that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
structure that bullets make scannable.

Surfaces that use this shape:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
  [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style).
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md` — see
  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.

Bullet *content* differs by surface:

- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
  mechanical edit list.
- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
  cases, invariants).

## Commit Message Style

input.old_string:
- **Title**: ≤50 chars (the "50" of the git 50/72 rule), a short
  summary of *what* changed. The version suffix counts toward the
  50 — the descriptive part gets whatever's left after `<type>: `
  and ` (<version>)`, so favor terse phrasings (`port X to Context`
  over `X → Context + XParams`) when the names run long. Common
  types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **Body**: [Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets), wrap
  ≤72 (the "72" of the 50/72 rule). App-repo bullets are
  **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file plus a
  one-line gist. This list **is the source of truth** for the
  cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the narrative +
  design, not a copy of it. Promote any "why" beyond one sentence
  to a chores `###` subsection rather than expanding the intro.
- **Session-repo body**: same shape; bullets describe in-session
  activity rather than code changes.
- Examples:

- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes

input.old_string:
### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
other.

input.old_string:
### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.
input.new_string:
### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

lines: Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
-comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
-[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).
+comment exists, not that it be long.
This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.
+**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
+a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
+paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
+that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:
+- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
+- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
+- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
+  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).
**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on

input.old_string:
## docs: chid+co -s/--scope flag (0.49.0-2.3)

The docs half of the `0.49.0-2` rollout. Code shipped in `-2.2`
but `README.md` still showed the pre-`-R`-single-path multi-repo
forms (`-R .,.claude` / `-R . -R .claude`) — anyone reading the
user-facing docs would be steered into invocations that no longer
parse. The cycle also surfaced a process drift: chores, todo, and
commit bodies all describe what amounts to the same intro+bullets
shape, but CLAUDE.md codified it in three almost-but-not-quite
places. Unified into one `## Prose form` section as the cycle's
second half. See [§ Design: Prose form unification](#design-prose-form-unification).

- `README.md` `### Multi-repo queries` rewritten to lead with
  `-s code,bot`; every example updated to the new form.
- `-R PATH` retained as the single-path escape hatch (`-R .`,
  `-R .claude`, `-R ../other`) and as the workspace-root
  override that composes with `-s` (`-R ../other -s code,bot`).
- Trailing paragraph forward-links to the `-s <path>` and
  `-s <path>,roles` future-Todos so readers see the deferred
  expressivity.
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` gains `resolve_repos` in the `common.rs`
  helper list with a one-line note on what it composes.
- `sync` flag table at the bottom of `README.md` left alone —
  `sync` still has the old repeatable/comma-list `-R`; its
  migration is queued under "Drop `-R` from `CommonArgs`".
- CLAUDE.md: new top-level `## Prose form` section is the single
  source of truth for the intro+bullets shape; `## Commit Message
  Style`, `### Chores section content`, and `### Doc comments…`
  slim to one-line references.

### Design: Prose form unification

Before this cycle CLAUDE.md described the same shape (short
intro + `-` bullet list, ≤72 wrap) three times — once inside
`### Doc comments…` (covering doc comments + chores + todo),
once inside `## Commit Message Style` (commit bodies, with the
50-col title add-on), once inside `### Chores section content`
("intro paragraph, what changed and *why*"). The drift between
them was small but real: only the Doc-comments copy carried the
explicit list of surfaces; only the Commit-Message copy carried
the file-by-file bullet rule; Done entries weren't mentioned
anywhere.

Three alternatives considered:

- **Expand the existing Shape rule's list** to add commits +
  Done. Smallest change; leaves the rule buried in `### Doc
  comments…` where it's hard to find when writing a commit body
  or chores section.
- **Cross-link three definitions to each other.** Each site
  redefines the shape but pointers run between them. Reads
  consistent but still drifts: three copies of the same
  paragraph still need three edits to stay in sync.
- **Promote to a single top-level `## Prose form` section**
  (chosen). The shape is defined once; the three (now four —
  Done entries) consumer sites carry only the surface-specific
  add-ons (50-col title for commits, file-by-file bullets for
  commits, `Commits:` line + design `###`s for chores, etc.).
  Anchors stay stable on the existing sections so notes/ refs
  don't churn.

The chosen form also distinguishes bullet *content* per
surface: commit-body bullets are file-by-file (the source of
truth for the mechanical edit list — `git show` is the record);
chores / todo / done bullets are conceptual (design points,
structural notes — never a copy of the commit's edit list);
doc-comment bullets are whatever structure fits.

message.content: lg good, but why undo the CLAUDE change? I would have thought you would have "stashed" it in another jj change, so why delete it?

content.text:
Fair call — I went linear (revert now, re-apply later) when jj makes it easy to keep multiple WIPs in flight.

The honest reasoning, split into two parts:

**The entangled bits *had* to be reverted either way** — the CLAUDE.md mention inside the `-2.3` chores intro and the prose-form bullet in `-2.3`'s todo entry weren't "stash candidates," they were content that belongs to `-2.3`'s description only after the prose-form work moves out. Reverting those was just cleanup.

**The pure additions could have been stashed** — the new `## Prose form` section in CLAUDE.md, the three slim-downs, the `### Design: Prose form unification` subsection in chores-10. None of those overlap `-2.3`'s edits. I could have `jj split` (or `jj new -B @` + move-the-additions-down) to land them as a separate `-2.4` commit sitting *above* `-2.3` in the chain, then pushed only `-2.3` first. That's the jj-native flow you're describing, and you're right it's cleaner.

Practically, nothing is actually lost — the content is in:

- **My conversation context** (the Edit tool calls show the exact `old_string`/`new_string` of every edit).
- **`jj op log`** for the app repo — every WC mutation is recorded; `jj op restore <op>` could resurrect any prior state. The CLAUDE.md + chores-10 Design subsection are in there as recently-undone states.

So re-applying for `-2.4` is fast either way. But your point stands: the jj-native move would have been to never delete in the first place. I'll lean into `jj split` (or sibling commits) the next time this comes up.

Want me to proceed with the `-2.3` push as planned, or pause to recover the prose-form work as a stashed `-2.4` commit *before* the `-2.3` push (via `jj op restore` or by re-applying it onto a child commit of `@`)? Either works; the re-apply is small.
